title: Setup
body:
Start the MCP server in a separate terminal first:

```
Terminal 1:  make serve         # mcpkit-Go fixture on :3101 by default
Terminal 2:  make demo          # this walkthrough (--tui for interactive TUI)
```

Point the walkthrough at a different host via either of:

```
go run . --demo --url http://localhost:1234       # CLI flag
MCPKIT_SERVER_URL=http://localhost:1234 make demo # env var
```

Any MCP host can connect to the running server (Claude Desktop, VS Code, MCPJam, basic-host). The walkthrough below acts as a scripted host that issues the protocol calls directly through `*mcpkit/client.Client` — no LLM, no browser, no JS. The same protocol calls drive the iframe when you run `make demo-app EXAMPLE=quickstart` in basic-host (see the [centralized guide](../README.md#other-ways-to-test-a-fixture)).

title: Connect to the fixture
output: connected to Quickstart MCP App Server 1.0.0

title: tools/list — verify get-time + its UI resource
tools/call get-time — the timestamp the iframe renders
output:
content[0]: type="text" text="2026-06-08T17:28:41.523874Z"
    structuredContent: <none>  (quickstart returns text-only)

title: resources/read — the iframe HTML the App ships in
output:
uri:      ui://get-time/mcp-app.html
    mimeType: text/html;profile=mcp-app
    bytes:    328192  (iframe HTML+JS bundle)
    _meta:    <none>  (no csp / permissions declared)

title: What the App iframe does with all this
body:
This is the point where a Go-only scripted host hits its limit: everything from here runs in the iframe's JavaScript via the MCP Apps bridge, not over the MCP wire.

When `make demo-app EXAMPLE=quickstart` runs this fixture inside basic-host, the host:

1. Calls `tools/call get-time` exactly as step 3 above.
2. Reads `ui://get-time/mcp-app.html` exactly as step 4 above.
3. Renders the iframe in a sandbox, loads the JS bundle, and pushes the tool result into the iframe via the [mcp-app-bridge](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/ext-apps/blob/main/src/app-bridge.ts) `postMessage` channel.

Inside the iframe — which is what you saw in step 4's bytes — quickstart's App SDK does the **bare minimum** for an MCP App:

- Sets `app.ontoolresult` to extract `result.content[0].text` and write it into the DOM as "Server Time: <ISO timestamp>".
- Wires a button click to call `app.callServerTool({name: "get-time", arguments: {}})` — the iframe calling BACK to the server through the bridge to fetch a fresh timestamp. This is the **simplest possible iframe-talks-to-server** pattern: no app-side tools registered, no model context push, just a button that re-runs the server tool.

That's it. Contrast with budget-allocator's iframe, which calls `app.registerTool` five times to expose `get-allocations` / `set-allocation` / `set-total-budget` / `set-company-stage` / `get-benchmark-comparison` to the model, and `app.updateModelContext` after every UI interaction. quickstart shows the FLOOR of what an MCP App can be — the bare-minimum bridge dance — which makes it the cleanest reference for understanding what differentiates an MCP App from "just an MCP server with a UI hint."

title: Where to look in the code
body:
- `main.go` — the fixture is ~60 lines: one TypedAppTool returning a text content block, one paired UI resource, CORS for browser hosts.
- `walkthrough.go` — this file. Each step's curl + Go recipe is the canonical wire reproduction.
- `../README.md` — narrative + screenshots + the [Other ways to test a fixture](../README.md#other-ways-to-test-a-fixture) guide.
- Upstream's iframe source: `/tmp/ext-apps/examples/quickstart/src/mcp-app.ts` — 26 lines of JS that show the minimum-viable bridge dance.
